title: General Care
content_markdown:
#### Patient Centred Care

Aim to provide safe, effective and compassionate patient‐centred care in all interactions, by: 

- Treating patients, carers and families with dignity and respect.
- Encouraging and supporting shared decision making by patients, their families and carers. 
- Communicating and sharing information with patients, their families and carers, and other. 
- Members of their healthcare team.
- Obtaining consent and considering patient wishes and values in all decisions. 

**Considerations for safe and effective patient care:**

- Ensuring safe and effective care is the responsibility of all ACTAS members who attend a patient. 
- All members of the treating team, regardless of training or clinical level, have the responsibility to raise any concerns about patient care in a timely fashion.
- Provide care within your scope of practice (as per Authority for and Scope of Clinical Practice – Ambulance Service Policy AS131).
- All medication must be verbally and visually cross‐checked prior to administration, wherever possible. - Be aware of, and respectfully manage expectations through effective communication.

#### Initial Actions

- Primary survey.
- Haemorrhage control.
- Posture.
- Oxygen therapy.

#### Monitoring and Assessment

**Monitor and assess as required / appropriate: **

- Vital signs 
- At least 2 sets, 5 minutes apart
- Heart rate, GCS, respiratory rate, BP, skin condition, SpO2.

- Cardiac monitor \xb1 12/15 lead ECG.
- Blood glucose.
- Temperature.
- Chest auscultation.
- Pain assessment.
- Mental competence assessment.
- EtCO2.
- Hydration assessment.
- Blood lactate.
- Mobilisation assessment.
- Specific observations and assessments as per patient condition.

#### Subsequent Actions

- **Treatment** as required (as per appropriate CMG(s)).
- Remember to verbally and visually **cross-check all medications** prior to administration, wherever possible.
- Identify and manage **time critical** presentations 
- Refer to Time Critical Patient Guideline
- **Note: time critical does not just mean rapid transport!** Minimum scene time, treatment en route (where possible) and prompt transport are all required for time critical patients.
- Facilitate or provide **transport** as required (notify hospital as appropriate) 
- Remember: **Transport is treatment!**
- **Communicate** patient and situational details to receiving facility, as appropriate 
- e.g. IMISTER‐AMBO handover protocol.
- Complete accurate and comprehensive **documentation.**

Glucose.text: Glucose 10% IV: Formula: 0.25 (2.5ml)/kg Preparation: 50g in 500ml Dose: 0.75ml/kg Volume: 7.5ml
Treatment of hypoglycaemia (BGL <4mmol/L) when patient is unable to eat or drink.
Hypertonic glucose solution – containing 0.1g dextrose per ml
10% glucose – in 250ml in collapsible plastic flask
Treat hypoglycaemia (BGL <4mmol/L, and patient unable to eat/drink)
Patients who respond to glucose 10% should eat, to prevent later development of further hypoglycaemia.
Treatment of hypoglycaemia (BGL <4mmol/L) when patient is conscious and able to swallow
1 x tube of 15g glucose orally  May be repeated once after 15 minutes if BGL still < 4 mmol/L, and level of consciousness still altered Total maximum dose 30g glucose

##### Type
Hypertonic glucose solution – containing 0.1g dextrose per ml

##### Presentations
10% glucose – in 250ml in collapsible plastic flask

##### Actions
Glucose is the main energy source for the body cells, especially the brain. 
 Onset: 30 – 60 seconds. 

#### Uses
Treat hypoglycaemia (BGL

##### Type
Oral glucose gel [no schedule]

##### Presentations
37.5g tube containing 15g glucose

##### Actions

- Glucose is the main energy source for the body’s cells, especially the brain 

- Glucose preparation is rapidly absorbed from oral / buccal mucosa 

- Causes an increase in blood glucose concentration

Onset: approximately 10 minutes.

#### Uses
Treatment of hypoglycaemia (BGL

#### Indications
- Altered level of consciousness.
- Suspected stroke.
- Post seizure.
- Diabetic.
#### Procedure

- Place strip into monitor. The glucometer will auto turn on.

- Select a finger and clean with an alcohol swab, allow finger to dry.

- Using a lancet, pierce the skin and squeeze a small amount of blood from the wound.

- Dispose of the used lancet appropriately.

- Place a sample of blood on the test strip until the BGL device beeps and a countdown appears.

- Apply a Band-Aid to the wound.

- Wait for the test result and record the BGL measurement.

- Dispose of the used test strip appropriately.

#### Specific Notes
- A “LO” or “HI” reading indicates a BGL 27.8 mmol/L.
- Presence of ^ arrow does not indicate ketones, this signals a high BGL reading. Ketone testing requires a different testing strip, this testing method will not give any indication of ketones.

Treat with 100% oxygen.

##### Type
A naturally occurring gas 

##### Presentations
Compressed gas in all-white cylinders of various sizes, to be fitted with an appropriate pressure-reducing device 

##### Actions
Essential element for aerobic metabolic needs and sustaining life. 
 Oxygen administration improves, or prevents a reduction in, the oxygen content of blood leaving the lungs. 

#### Uses
A wide range of situations, for the treatment or prevention of hypoxaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Increased risk of fire / explosion – oxygen strongly supports combustion 

- Mucosal dryness and irritation

- Hypoventilation in some COPD patients with CO2 retention (titrate oxygen to maintain SpO2 88 – 92%)

#### Contraindications

- Known paraquat poisoning with SpO2 ≥88%

- History of bleomycin therapy with SpO2 ≥88%

#### Precautions
Neonates (especially pre-term infants) are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of oxygen. When absolutely required, the lowest effective concentration, for the shortest possible time, should be used to achieve adequate oxygenation.

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
With the exception of conditions listed below, oxygen should generally be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥94% (i.e, unless otherwise required, there is no need to push saturations to 100%). 

Deliver oxygen through a device appropriate to the required inspired concentration of oxygen.

- **Nasal cannulae: **1 – 4 litres/min (approx. 24 – 40% oxygen) for low oxygen requirements
- **Oxygen (‘Hudson’) mask: **6 – 15 litres/min (approx. 30 – 65% oxygen) for moderate oxygen requirements
- **Nebuliser: **8 litres/min for moderate oxygen requirements with need for aerosolised medications- **Non-rebreather mask (NRBM): **15 litres/min (approx. 60 – 97% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Bag-valve-mask (BVM): **15 litres/min or demand valve (up to 100% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): **8 – 15 litres/min (titrated to achieve desired effect/pressure) for high oxygen requirements.
#### Conditions with specific oxygen requirement
- Conditions requiring 100% oxygen delivery (NRBM, BVM) 
- Obstetric emergencies
- Carbon monoxide poisoning
- Unrelieved upper airway obstruction
- Diving emergencies.
- COPD patients with possible CO2 retention – titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 of 88 – 92%.
- Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) – aim for highest SpO2 possible 
- High flow oxygen (15 litres/min) via nasal cannulae is mandatory during this procedure, in addition to IPPV.

#### Indications
- For patients that require supplemental oxygen.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

##### Nasal Prongs (minimal oxygen requirements – approx 24-40%)

- Attach nasal prong tubing to the oxygen port.

- Select oxygen flow rate. 
- **Note: 15 Lpm continuous during intubation.**

- Place one nasal prong in each nostril and tubing tucked behind each ear.

- Position the slide adjuster underneath the patient’s chin.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 2

##### Hudson Mask (moderate oxygen requirements – approx 30-65%)

- Attach the oxygen tubing to the Hudson mask.

- Connect the oxygen tubing to the oxygen port.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the metal nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 3

##### Non-Rebreather Mask (high oxygen requirements - approx 60-97%)

- Prepare as for Hudson mask.

- Select oxygen flow rate of >10 Lpm.

- Ensure reservoir bag is inflated.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.
